worth_it_explanation: Awareness-focused DV360 campaigns are routinely measured against click-through metrics that have no meaningful relationship to whether anyone noticed or remembered the ad. These checks confirm that the measurement setup matches the campaign goal, that reach is counted in people rather than raw impressions, and that any third-party measurement running alongside DV360 is reconciled rather than left to contradict it silently. The fixes are configuration changes inside DV360 and Campaign Manager 360 (CM360), not rebuilds, and they make the post-campaign story credible.
intro: Brand lift and reach reporting in Display & Video 360 (DV360) gives awareness advertisers a measurement path beyond clicks and conversions. Brand lift surveys, run through Google, measure the change in awareness, consideration, or purchase intent that a campaign produces. Unique reach reports count distinct people reached across screens rather than summing impressions. Together they let an advertiser answer whether the campaign reached the right people and whether it moved them. These checks confirm that awareness goals are paired with the right measurement tools, that unique reach is the unit of analysis rather than raw impression volume, and that DV360 measurement is reconciled against any verification or third-party measurement running concurrently.

audit_checks.title: Awareness-goal campaigns measure success by clicks rather than brand lift or viewability
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign designed to build awareness produces no useful signal when measured by click-through rate. The team sees a metric that was never connected to the goal, draws no conclusion about whether the campaign worked, and has no data to improve the next flight.

educate.base:
Display & Video 360 (DV360) supports two distinct measurement paths for campaigns that run awareness or consideration goals rather than direct response. The first is brand lift measurement, a Google-run survey study that exposes a control group to no ads and a treatment group to the campaign, then measures the difference in awareness, ad recall, consideration, or purchase intent between the two groups. The second is Active View viewability, which reports the share of impressions where the ad was visible on screen for a qualifying duration. Both measure something connected to whether the ad had a chance to work. Click-through rate measures whether someone took an action the ad was not designed to produce.

When an awareness campaign is evaluated on click-through rate or cost per click, the numbers will look low because display and video ads at the top of the funnel are not designed to drive clicks. The campaign may be performing exactly as intended, but the metric makes it look like it is failing. That misread leads to budget cuts, creative changes, or targeting adjustments aimed at a problem that does not exist, while the actual performance against the awareness goal stays unknown.

Brand lift studies through DV360 require a minimum spend threshold to produce statistically valid results. Campaigns below that threshold can fall back to Active View viewability as the primary awareness signal. You may want to confirm which measurement path is available given the campaign's budget before the flight begins, so the measurement setup is in place before impressions are delivered.

A few signs this check applies to a campaign:
  • The campaign objective is set to 'Brand awareness and reach' or a similar awareness label, but the primary key performance indicator (KPI) in the insertion order (IO) is set to click-through rate or cost per click
  • No brand lift study is attached to the campaign or advertiser, and Active View viewability is not being tracked at the IO or line item (LI) level
  • Post-campaign reporting references only clicks and conversions for a flight that ran display or video at scale
You may want to confirm the KPI on each IO reflects the goal the campaign was designed to achieve before reading performance data from it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running an upper-funnel display or connected TV (CTV) campaign to build brand awareness before a sale period, measuring by click-through rate will show poor results because the goal is reach and recall, not immediate purchase. You may want to confirm the IO KPI is set to viewability or that a brand lift study is attached if the spend qualifies.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running awareness display ahead of a demand-capture campaign, clicks from the awareness flight are not the right signal. You may want to confirm the awareness IO uses an Active View or brand lift KPI and that the demand-capture campaign downstream is tracked separately.

learn_more.source_title: About brand lift measurement in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Review the KPI setting on each IO flagged as awareness-goal, confirm whether a brand lift study exists, and note whether Active View viewability is tracked.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser and navigate to Campaigns. For each campaign with an awareness or reach objective, open the IOs under it.
In each IO's settings, find the 'Performance goal' or KPI field. Note whether it is set to a viewability metric, a brand lift metric, or a click- or conversion-based metric.
Check whether a brand lift study is listed under the advertiser's Measurement settings. If a study exists, confirm it is linked to this campaign.
If no brand lift study is present, open a sample of LIs and confirm that Active View viewability reporting is enabled and that the viewability goal is set.

campaign_manager_360.label: Campaign Manager 360 (CM360)
steps: In CM360, open the campaign and review the Floodlight activities assigned to it. Confirm whether any brand lift activity type is present.
Check the campaign's reporting settings to confirm whether Active View columns are included in the default report.

evaluate.logic: For each IO with an awareness or reach objective, the practitioner records the KPI setting and whether a brand lift study or Active View tracking is in place. An IO with a viewability KPI and Active View tracking confirmed, or with a brand lift study attached, passes. An IO with a click-through or conversion KPI and no brand lift or viewability measurement is a fail, recorded with the IO name and the KPI found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the KPI setting and measurement status for each awareness-goal IO above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that awareness-goal campaigns on {{site_name}} are measured against click-through rate rather than brand lift or viewability. Display and video campaigns designed to build awareness do not produce clicks at meaningful rates, so a click-based KPI reports poor performance on a campaign that may be working as designed. {{voice_recommend}} setting the performance goal on each awareness IO to a viewability metric, attaching a brand lift study to campaigns where the spend qualifies, and separating awareness and direct-response reporting so each flight is judged against its own objective.

audit_checks.title: Reach is reported in raw impressions rather than unique reach by person
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Raw impression volume counts every ad server call, including multiple exposures to the same person on multiple devices. Using it as the reach number overstates how many distinct people the campaign reached and understates frequency, which can mask overexposure to a narrow audience while the campaign appears to be reaching many.

educate.base:
Display & Video 360 (DV360) provides unique reach reporting that estimates the number of distinct people a campaign reached across devices and formats, using Google's signed-in user graph. This is separate from impression volume. A single person who sees an ad five times on a phone and twice on a laptop contributes seven impressions but one unique reach count.

When reach reporting uses raw impressions, the campaign appears to have touched far more people than it did. The effective frequency per person, meaning the number of times the ad reached the same individual, is also understated. A campaign that delivered 10 million impressions to 500,000 people averaged 20 exposures per person, a level of frequency that may cause ad fatigue or budget waste, but that fact is invisible when the metric shown is only total impressions.

DV360's unique reach metrics appear in the 'Reach & Frequency' reporting section and include unique reach, average impression frequency, and on-target percentage when demographic targeting is active. These metrics are modeled estimates, not deterministic counts, but they produce a much more accurate picture of audience coverage than raw impressions alone.

A few signs this check applies:
  • Post-campaign or in-flight reports show only impressions and CPM (cost per thousand impressions) with no unique reach or frequency column
  • The IO or campaign report does not include the 'Unique reach' or 'Average impression frequency' columns
  • Frequency caps are set at the IO or LI level but the team has no report confirming whether those caps are holding
You may want to confirm that the standard reporting template for awareness campaigns includes unique reach and average frequency before the next flight begins.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a broad awareness campaign across display and connected TV (CTV), unique reach tells you how many households or individuals the campaign reached, while average frequency tells you whether the same households were hit repeatedly. You may want to confirm both columns are in the post-campaign report before making creative rotation or budget decisions.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser building awareness before a retargeting phase, unique reach confirms how large the primed audience actually is. You may want to confirm the unique reach number before estimating the expected retargeting pool size.

learn_more.source_title: About unique reach reporting in Display & Video 360
Set up and manage frequency caps

investigate.intro: Confirm that the campaign's reach reports include unique reach and average frequency columns, not only impression volume.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser and navigate to Reports. Locate any report used for the awareness campaign in question.
Check whether the report includes 'Unique reach', 'Average impression frequency', or 'Unique cookies' columns alongside impressions.
If no such columns exist, open the report editor and confirm whether they are available to add. The 'Reach & Frequency' metric group in DV360 reporting contains these columns.
Check whether a frequency cap is set on the relevant IOs and LIs, and note the cap value alongside the average frequency from the report to see if the cap is holding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the campaign's standard reach report includes unique reach and average impression frequency columns. A report that includes both passes. A report that shows only impressions and CPM with no unique reach column is a fail, recorded with the report name and the columns currently present.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the columns present in the campaign reach report above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV360 reach reporting relies on raw impression volume rather than unique reach by person. Impression counts include multiple exposures to the same individual, so the reported reach number overstates distinct audience coverage and makes average frequency appear lower than it is. {{voice_recommend}} updating the standard awareness campaign report template to include 'Unique reach' and 'Average impression frequency' from the DV360 Reach and Frequency metric group, and reviewing frequency cap settings on the affected IOs against the actual average frequency the report shows.

audit_checks.title: DV360 measurement is not reconciled against verification or third-party measurement running concurrently
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When DV360 and a third-party measurement vendor run on the same campaign without reconciliation, discrepancies between the two go unexplained. The team may act on the wrong number or lose confidence in both sources without understanding which discrepancy is expected and which signals a real problem.

educate.base:
Many DV360 campaigns run a third-party measurement vendor, such as Nielsen Digital Ad Ratings, Comscore Campaign Ratings, or a brand lift vendor like Kantar or Lucid, alongside DV360's own measurement. Each vendor counts impressions, reach, and frequency using its own methodology, so the numbers they report will not match DV360's numbers exactly. Some discrepancy is expected. Unreconciled discrepancy is a problem.

Known sources of expected discrepancy include differences in cookie or identifier matching, differences in how each vendor handles server-to-server versus browser-based measurement, and differences in how each vendor counts viewable impressions. A 10 to 20 percent difference between DV360 and a third-party vendor is within a range that practitioners typically accept as methodological. A 40 percent difference, or a difference that grows week over week, points to a real configuration issue worth investigating.

Reconciliation means documenting the expected discrepancy range at the campaign's start, reviewing both sources at a consistent cadence during the flight, and noting when a discrepancy falls outside that range so it can be traced to a cause. Without this, a large discrepancy discovered at the end of a campaign has no baseline to compare against and no way to determine when it started.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A third-party measurement tag or pixel is attached to the campaign but no reconciliation baseline was documented at launch
  • Post-campaign reports from DV360 and the third-party vendor show substantially different impression or reach numbers and there is no explanation on file for the difference
  • The campaign uses both DV360 brand lift and a third-party brand lift vendor, and the results are reported separately without noting whether the methodology difference accounts for the delta
You may want to confirm a reconciliation baseline exists and is reviewed at a set interval rather than only at the campaign's end.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a brand campaign with both DV360 viewability measurement and a Nielsen or Comscore study for on-target reach, you may want to confirm that the expected discrepancy between the two vendors was documented before the campaign launched so mid-flight or post-campaign variance has a reference point.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running a DV360 awareness campaign alongside a third-party brand lift study, you may want to confirm that the measurement vendors' methodologies were compared at setup so the post-campaign results from each can be read alongside each other rather than in conflict.

learn_more.source_title: About Floodlight and Campaign Manager 360 reporting

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a third-party measurement vendor is running alongside DV360, whether a reconciliation baseline was documented, and whether discrepancies have been reviewed during the flight.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser's Measurement settings and check whether any third-party measurement vendors are listed as active.
For the campaign in question, open the creative trafficking details and confirm whether a third-party impression or viewability tag is attached to the creatives.
Ask the team whether a reconciliation baseline, meaning the expected discrepancy range between DV360 and the third-party vendor, was documented at the campaign's start.

campaign_manager_360.label: CM360
steps: In CM360, open the placement and creative settings to confirm whether a third-party tracking tag or verification vendor tag is attached.
Pull a delivery report from CM360 and compare total impressions against the DV360 delivery report for the same date range. Note the percentage difference and compare it to any documented baseline.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a third-party measurement vendor is active on the campaign and, if so, whether a reconciliation baseline was documented and reviewed at a consistent cadence. A campaign with a third-party vendor, a documented baseline, and evidence of in-flight reconciliation passes. A campaign with a third-party vendor but no documented baseline or no reconciliation review is a fail, recorded with the vendor name and the percentage discrepancy found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a third-party measurement vendor is active, whether a baseline was documented, and the observed discrepancy percentage above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV360 campaigns run alongside third-party measurement without a reconciliation baseline or regular review of the discrepancy between sources. When DV360 and a third-party vendor report different impression or reach numbers without context, the team cannot distinguish expected methodological variance from a real configuration problem. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the expected discrepancy range between DV360 and the third-party vendor before each campaign launches, reviewing both sources at a consistent cadence during the flight, and noting any variance that falls outside the expected range so it can be investigated while the campaign is still running.
